They've heard about it in the news, they've heard it's risky. So for us to say up front, we're selling you a commodities course, nobody is going to buy. We have to first get them over that hurdle and sell them on the opportunity, sell them on the business, and once we've done that, then we can tell them what it is, because it already pre-sold at that point. So let me show you a few things in here. I'm going to go over about four points. The first one is, turn to page six. So instead of talking about a commodity, instead of talking about commodity option course, we're going to talk about Mike H. And how he made three-year salary in only three months. We're going to talk about Dick B. is ready to pay off his mortgage. Now does that sound like something you guys want to buy into? If you're a prospect? Yeah, those are great things. We go on to tell him if you flip the page over to page nine, you're going to see a picture of Arrow who's the owner, and we've got a picture of him and his dog. Do you think there's a reason the dog is in the photo? Yeah, it makes him look more ordinary. Makes him look like the average customer. We tried him in a striped suit. We tried him in a striped shirt. We tried him in a plain shirt. We tried him standing, sitting down. We found out the one with the dog was the one that got people to respond the best. OK, so let's go over to page 11. On page 11, we're going to tell a story about a Greek philosopher who came up with this wonderful business opportunity hundreds of years ago, and it's still in existence today, and that you can still profit and make money from this unique business. OK, still not telling what it is, just giving some information from it. Last thing I'm going to show you in this piece is the bottom of page 12. And it says, a few years ago, I took my dad car shopping. This is Arrow, the owner. We want to show that he's made a lot of money from this business. So he's going to tell this story. A few years ago, I took my dad car shopping, pick out whatever you want, I said, and he drove home in his new $30,000 sob automobile. So Arrow and I had a little disagreement on this. He wanted to keep the name of the automobile sob in there, and I said, Arrow, we can't do that. It's going to lower response. It's like Tuttlevin when you just bought your dad a fiat or a Volkswagen. And he's a sob guy, so he says, no, we're going to keep it in there. So I lost that battle. But you would keep reading this piece. You'd keep hearing stories. You'd keep seeing testimonials. And you would wait until you're about 24 or 25 pages into it before we told you what the offer is, so that we could diffuse the risk, or in this case, remove any preconceived ideas. So this piece does the same thing that Kinz does. It does the same thing that Rust Always piece does, as we present the offer in a way that diffuses the risk. We focus on the benefits, and it connects with the customer on their level. We tell a story about Arrow and how he grew up, and how he had a modest upbringing. How he rode his bike to school uphill both ways through a foot of snow. You know, the whole thing. We made him look very average and very normal. So when Kin was selling his course for $100, a good question, by the way. Yes. The question was, when Kin Roberts was selling his course, it was for $195 a piece. This version of this booklet here is $59. Why the decrease in price? When Kin was selling the course, there was not very much competition out there. There was very little. Before the internet, it was before a lot of people had jumped on this bandwagon of selling financial offers through the mail. And so since then, if you were to go online and do a search, or look in the mailbox to see how many other offers there are out there for this type of financial business opportunity product, there are dozens and dozens and dozens and dozens of them. So the market is much more saturated than it used to be, and we've had to lower our price in order to stay competitive. How does that affect the number? I'll talk about the numbers at the end. There's some very good things on the numbers I'll show you guys at the end. So basically, I've showed you guys a few different samples. You can see how presenting the offer differently can increase response rates, can change things. The second thing I want to talk to you about is the driving force behind the Kin Roberts Company. It was direct mail. We sold out of the 700,000 courses, 95% to 99% of them were sold through direct mail. The other 1% to 5% was sold through Card Dex, Radio, and some print ads. So we used direct mail heavily. We had to be very targeted in the list we took, and I'm going to review some mailing lists from you in just a few minutes. And we had to know exactly who we were going to. We had to know exactly who our customers and prospects were. The largest mailing I did for Kin Roberts was 5.7 million pieces. It's a lot of mail. That's one mailing, one drop. It took a mail up in the state of Washington. They had to make the paper, and they had to ship it down to my printer in LA. And the printer was the same printer that prints the West Coast edition of the New York Times. It's a big, big printing facility. And they shipped the paper down on train, and it took 36 boxcars full of paper to get from point A to point B so that we could send this mailing out. It's a lot of paper. And I want to tell people that. I cringe a little bit because people worry you're wasting all that paper. And part of it was recycled. And the other thing I say is I'm from Oregon, and we have so many trees there that we can't cut that people are actually living in the trees with the owls, which is true. If we actually have a treehouse hotel about 45 minutes from my house that you can go check in. I've never been there. I won't go there, but it's what people have resorted to. So anyways, we're going to talk a little bit about direct mail lists. How many of you guys have done direct mail for yourself? Let's see you show our hands. Good, a lot of you. How many of you have been successful in direct mail? Good. All right, great. That's wonderful. So you guys know that there's a few key things to direct mail. You got the copy, you got the list, and you got the offer. So we're going to talk a little about lists. And these lists are what really gave us a huge ability to reach out to a broad universe, because we were targeted and who we were going to. I'm going to show you a response rate to a campaign. This is a campaign response rate that Frank Kern got. I asked him if I could share this with you guys, and he said I could. And he did say this. He said, as long as you hug Dan every time you see him, he loves that. That's a quote from Frank. Now, I'm going to let him and Dan work that out. But when Frank did his first direct mail campaign this last fall, this was the response rate he got when he went to a prospect list. Not his house file, but a prospect list. 0.05%. And I didn't work with him. I don't know how many pieces he mailed. But if he mailed 5,000 pieces, he would have gotten two and a half responses. Not very good. On a second campaign, I helped him pick a list. And his response rate was 2%. Significant difference, right? So if he was to mail 5,000 pieces the second time, he would have gotten 100 responses.
